Name: Stakeholder index
Definition: Index identifying a stakeholder agent in the ecosystem
Equation / Rule: i ∈ A, where A is the set of stakeholder agents
Interpretation: Distinguishes students, vendors, suppliers, delivery actors, university, operator, and regulator
Campus Food Example: i = Vendor_A or Students_1

Name: Simulation time step
Definition: Discrete time index used to track mission intensity and ecosystem state
Equation / Rule: t advances by Δt in each simulation step
Interpretation: Allows stakeholder missions to be represented as time-varying functions
Campus Food Example: t may represent one day of campus food operation
Simulation Placeholder: Default: daily step; can be changed to hourly

Name: Time-step resolution
Definition: Duration represented by one simulation step
Interpretation: Determines the granularity of mission planning and synchronization
Campus Food Example: Δt = 1 day for strategic simulation or Δt = 1 hour for lunch-period simulation

Name: Stakeholder capacity
Definition: Maximum mission-relevant capacity of stakeholder i
Unit: meals/time step, deliveries/time step, policy actions/time step, normalized units
Equation / Rule: C_i is specified by case parameters or calibration data
Interpretation: Defines the maximum feasible mission workload for a stakeholder
Campus Food Example: Vendor_A capacity = 180 meals/day

Name: Mission intensity
Definition: Planned mission execution intensity of stakeholder i at time t
Allowable Range: 0 ≤ M_i(t) ≤ 1; values >1 may represent overload in stress tests
Interpretation: Measures how strongly a stakeholder commits capacity to its mission
Campus Food Example: M_vendor(t)=0.85 means vendor plans to use 85% of capacity

Name: Baseline mission intensity
Definition: Mission intensity generated by local, independent stakeholder planning
Interpretation: Represents independent stakeholder behavior without digital platform or PUDAL coordination
Campus Food Example: Vendor estimates production from yesterday's sales

Name: Online-platform mission intensity
Definition: Mission intensity informed by platform transaction and demand signals
Interpretation: Represents partially improved planning using digital transaction visibility
Campus Food Example: Vendor adjusts production using pre-order data

Name: MOEE mission intensity
Definition: Mission intensity coordinated through PUDAL and MSRS
Allowable Range: 0 ≤ M_i^MOEE(t) ≤ 1; overload values may be allowed for crisis scenarios
Interpretation: Represents mission execution after ecosystem-level coordination
Campus Food Example: Vendor, supplier, and delivery intensity are synchronized before demand surge

Name: Mission workload
Definition: Actual planned workload implied by mission intensity
Unit: meals, deliveries, inspections, service units per time step
Interpretation: Converts normalized mission intensity into operational workload
Campus Food Example: If C_vendor=180 and M_vendor=0.80, W_vendor=144 meals

Name: Required resources
Definition: Resources required by stakeholder i to execute mission intensity at time t
Unit: ingredient units, labor hours, IDR, compute units
Interpretation: Links mission intensity to resource requirements in the RMOV account
Campus Food Example: Higher vendor mission intensity requires more ingredients and labor

Name: Mission output
Definition: Output produced by stakeholder i at time t
Unit: meals, deliveries, policy actions, recommendations, reports
Interpretation: Connects mission execution to output production
Campus Food Example: Vendor output equals meals produced and served

Name: Mission productivity coefficient
Definition: Efficiency with which mission workload becomes useful output
Interpretation: Captures stakeholder productivity or conversion efficiency
Campus Food Example: Vendor productivity declines when supply shock reduces ingredient quality

Name: Resource coefficient
Definition: Resource amount required per unit of mission workload
Interpretation: Determines how much resource is consumed when mission intensity increases
Campus Food Example: Ingredient units required per meal produced

Name: Ecosystem demand
Definition: Total demand that must be served by the ecosystem at time t
Unit: meals/time step or service units/time step
Interpretation: Represents external or user-side mission pressure on the ecosystem
Campus Food Example: Total student meal demand during lunch period

Name: Ecosystem state
Definition: State vector observed by PUDAL for coordination
Interpretation: Collects the information needed for ecosystem-level decision making
Campus Food Example: Demand, vendor capacity, supplier availability, waste, satisfaction, and EVI

Name: Stakeholder recommendation
Definition: Recommendation issued by MSRS to stakeholder i at time t
Interpretation: Mechanism for influencing stakeholder mission intensity while preserving autonomy
Campus Food Example: Recommend Vendor_A to produce at 90% capacity

Name: Recommendation acceptance indicator
Definition: Whether stakeholder i accepts the MSRS recommendation
Interpretation: Models stakeholder autonomy and willingness to follow ecosystem recommendations
Campus Food Example: Vendor accepts production recommendation if expected benefit exceeds cost

Name: Mission synchronization index
Definition: Degree of alignment among stakeholder mission intensities at time t
Interpretation: Higher values indicate better synchronization across missions
Campus Food Example: High when vendor, supplier, and delivery missions peak together before lunch

Name: Mission bottleneck indicator
Definition: Indicates whether stakeholder i constrains ecosystem mission achievement at time t
Interpretation: Identifies stakeholders whose output is insufficient for downstream missions
Campus Food Example: Supplier bottleneck occurs when ingredient delivery is below vendor requirement

Name: Mission goal vector
Definition: Target mission outcomes for the ecosystem at time t
Interpretation: Represents mission goals used by PUDAL and MSRS
Campus Food Example: Nutrition ≥ 0.75; WasteReduction ≥ 0.80; EVI ≥ 0.85

Name: Ecosystem Value Index
Definition: Composite ecosystem performance score linked to mission intensity outcomes
Interpretation: Measures whether synchronized mission intensity produces systemic value
Campus Food Example: EVI improves when fulfillment, satisfaction, sustainability, and mission achievement rise
